meta.description: Medic career questline — from back-alley street doc to Chief of Medicine, 100 nodes spanning triage, moral reckoning, forbidden stack surgery, and the weight of holding life in your hands

jmed_001.title: The First Call
text: The call comes at three in the morning, the way the worst ones always do. A voice on a scratchy comm channel, breathless and young: "Someone's been cut bad. Real bad. Foundry accident. They said you do medicine."

You do medicine. That is one way to put it. You have a bag of salvaged instruments, a working knowledge of human anatomy learned from necessity, and steady hands. In the Lower District, that makes you a doctor. The fact that you have no license, no clinic, and no anesthesia beyond a bottle of grain alcohol is a detail the dying tend to overlook.

The address is a converted shipping container in Block 14, where the overhead pipes weep rust-colored condensation and the air tastes like machine oil. You find your patient on a table made from a door laid across two sawhorses. He is a foundry worker, mid-forties, with a laceration across his abdomen that has exposed things that should remain interior.

choices.text: Get to work immediately — every second counts
Assess the wound carefully before touching anything

jmed_002.title: Hands in the Dark
text: You don't waste time. Gloves on, light angled, hands inside the wound before the man can finish screaming. The grain alcohol serves double duty — disinfectant for the wound, anesthetic for the patient. He drinks. You pour. Together you make it work.

The laceration is deep but clean. A cutting tool, not a crush injury. The intestine is nicked but not perforated. You suture with fishing line because that is what you have, and your stitches are small and even because you practiced on synthetic skin until your fingers bled.

Forty minutes later, the man is unconscious from pain and alcohol, his abdomen closed, his chances upgraded from certain death to merely terrible. His wife stands in the doorway holding a child who watches you with enormous eyes.

choices.text: "He'll need antibiotics. I'll find some."
"Keep the wound clean. I'll check on him tomorrow."

jmed_003.title: The Careful Hand
text: You force yourself to slow down. Panic is the enemy. You catalog the wound systematically: depth, extent, organs involved, blood loss rate. The man watches you with eyes that have gone glassy with shock, and you recognize the look — he has already decided he is dying.

"You're not dying," you tell him, and you say it with the conviction of someone who will not allow it. "The cut is clean. Your organs are intact. I'm going to close this and you're going to be complaining about the scar in a week."

It is not entirely true — the intestine has a nick that could become peritonitis if left untreated — but the lie serves its purpose. His breathing steadies. His hands unclench from the table edge. Hope is the first medicine, and it costs nothing.

You work methodically, cleaning, suturing, closing. The fishing line holds. The wound seals. By the time you're done, the man is sleeping, which is the body's way of saying it believes it might survive after all.

choices.text: Leave instructions with his wife and head out into the rain
Stay with the patient until you're sure he's stable. Instructions aren't enough

jmed_004.title: Word Spreads
text: Word travels fast in the Lower District. By the next evening, three more people find you. A child with a fever that won't break. A woman with a cough that brings up blood. An old man whose hands shake so badly he can no longer feed himself.

None of them can pay. Not really. The child's mother offers a jar of preserves. The woman with the cough gives you a blanket that smells of smoke but is warm. The old man has nothing to offer and the shame of it sits on his face like a wound you cannot suture.

You treat them all. The child needs fluids and rest. The woman needs an X-ray you cannot provide and antibiotics you don't have. The old man needs a neurologist, a luxury so far beyond the Lower District's reach it might as well exist on another planet.
This is medicine down here. Not healing. Triage. You sort the dying from the salvageable and do what you can for both, knowing it will never be enough.

choices.text: Focus on the cases you can actually help
Try to find real medicine on the black market

jmed_005.title: Triage
text: You focus on what is possible. The child recovers with hydration and time. The woman with the cough — you give her what palliative care you can and tell her to rest, knowing she will go back to the textile works tomorrow because rest is a luxury she cannot afford.

The old man dies three days later. His neighbor finds him in his container, hands finally still. You hear about it secondhand, and something in your chest tightens — not guilt exactly, but something close to it. The knowledge that you triaged him into the 'nothing to be done' column and moved on.
A runner from the Bone Saints gang finds you that evening. Their enforcer took a bullet in a turf fight. They'll pay real credits. They'll also expect discretion.

choices.text: Treat the gang enforcer — credits are credits
Refuse gang work on principle

jmed_006.title: The Black Market
text: The black market operates in the spaces between walls — literally. Merchants set up in the gaps between shipping containers, in maintenance corridors, in the crawlspaces beneath the elevated transit lines. You find a pharmaceutical dealer named Kess who sells expired corporate medicine at a tenth of the original price.
"Antibiotics, broad-spectrum," you tell her. "Anti-inflammatories. Analgesics. Whatever you've got for neurological tremor."

Kess looks at you with the flat assessment of someone who has survived by reading people accurately. "You're that street doc everyone's talking about. The one who sewed up Tomasz with fishing line." She names a price that would clean out your savings.
"I can do half," you say.
"You can do three-quarters," she says. "And I'll throw in a box of surgical gloves. Real ones, not the recycled kind that tear if you look at them wrong."

choices.text: Pay the three-quarters price
Try to negotiate further

jmed_007.title: The Gang's Doctor
text: The enforcer is a woman named Drava, built like a freight loader, with a bullet lodged in her shoulder that has shattered the clavicle into three pieces. She sits on a crate in a warehouse basement, surrounded by Bone Saints who watch you with the particular intensity of people who will hurt you very badly if their friend dies.
"You the doc?" Drava asks through gritted teeth.
"I'm what you've got."

The bullet extraction takes an hour. Drava refuses the grain alcohol and bites down on a leather strap instead, and the only sound she makes is a low, continuous hum that vibrates through her chest like a engine running at idle. When you pull the bullet free and begin setting the bone fragments with improvised splints, she watches your hands with professional interest.
"Good hands," she says when you're done. "Steady. You ever think about working full-time for us?"
The Bone Saints' lieutenant drops a credstick on the table. More money than you've seen in weeks.

choices.text: "I treat anyone who needs it. I don't belong to any gang."
"I'll keep you on my list. You keep me on yours."

jmed_008.title: Principles
text: You refuse the gang's runner. They look at you like you've spoken a language they don't understand, and then they leave.
That night, someone leaves a dead rat on your doorstep. A message. You throw it away and go back to work.

The next day, a different gang's runner finds you. The Iron Chain. Their fighter has a broken jaw from an arena bout. They heard you turned down the Bone Saints, and they respect that. Or at least they respect the leverage it gives them.
"We pay better," the runner says. "And we don't leave dead animals on people's doors."
In the end, the gangs find you whether you want them to or not. The only question is which ones, and on what terms.

choices.text: Treat the Iron Chain fighter — at least they asked politely
Refuse again. You treat civilians, not combatants

jmed_009.title: A Reputation Forms
text: Over the next weeks, your reputation crystallizes. You are the street doc of Block 14. The one who shows up at three in the morning. The one whose stitches hold. The one who doesn't ask questions about how the wound happened, or who made it, or why.

Your patient load doubles, then triples. You treat stab wounds and industrial burns, malnutrition and addiction, broken bones and broken spirits. You learn to sleep in forty-minute intervals because that is how long it takes between emergencies.

One night, you lose a patient. A teenager caught in a gang crossfire, both lungs punctured, blood filling his chest faster than you can drain it. He dies looking at you with an expression that is not accusation but something worse — trust. He trusted you to save him, and you couldn't.
You sit on the floor of your container afterward, hands still red, and stare at the wall until the shaking stops.

choices.text: Keep going. There are more patients who need you
Take a moment to grieve before continuing

jmed_010.title: The Line You Draw
text: You refuse again. The Iron Chain runner shrugs and leaves without threat or dead animal. Small mercies.

But the refusal costs you. Word gets around that the Block 14 doc is particular. The gangs stop sending runners. That means fewer credits, but it also means something else: the civilians come more freely. Mothers bring their children without looking over their shoulders. Old men come without fear of being seen entering a gang-affiliated space.
You have drawn a line, and the line has consequences in both directions. You are poorer and cleaner and the people who come to you now come because they need a doctor, not a co-conspirator.

Then one night a child arrives at your door, carried by her father, and the child has been shot. The father is Bone Saints. The child is six years old. She is bleeding from her stomach and she is looking at you with eyes that do not understand what is happening to her.

choices.text: Treat the child immediately — she's a child, not a gang member
Treat the child, but tell the father this changes nothing about your policy

jmed_011.title: The Night That Never Ends
text: A chemical spill at the foundry sends eighteen casualties to your door in a single night. Chemical burns, respiratory failure, one man whose eyes have been seared shut by an alkaline splash. You have supplies for perhaps four patients.
Triage becomes mathematics. Who can survive without treatment. Who will die regardless. Who sits in that narrow window where your intervention is the difference between living and not.

You divide them into three groups with chalk marks on their foreheads — green for stable, yellow for treatable, red for beyond help. A woman in the red group watches you mark her and says nothing. She already knows what the mark means.

choices.text: Focus on the yellow group — maximize lives saved
Try to save everyone, even the reds — you refuse to write anyone off

jmed_012.title: The Mathematics of Survival
text: You focus on the yellow group and save six of them. The greens survive on their own. The reds die, one by one, in the corners of your container, and you do not let yourself look at them while you work because if you look you will stop and if you stop someone in the yellow group will join the reds.

By dawn, the floor is covered in blood and chemical residue and the particular silence of a room that held dying people. You sit among the survivors and the dead and count: twelve alive, six dead. A ratio. A number. Not a victory.

Dr. Okafor from the Sector 7 clinic arrives with the first light. He surveys the scene, counts the living and the dead, and looks at you with recognition. Not admiration — recognition. The look of a man who has done this calculation himself and carries the same weight.
"You did well," he says.
"Six people are dead."
"Twelve people are alive. That's what well looks like down here."

choices.text: "I need better supplies. Real equipment. Or the next spill kills more."
"Teach me. I need to know more if I'm going to do this."

jmed_013.title: Refusing the Math
text: You refuse to triage. Every patient gets your attention. You run between them, stitching, treating, stabilizing, moving on. You pour every ounce of energy and every scrap of supply into the fight against mathematics itself.

The math wins. It always does. Three of the yellow group die while you are trying to save a red. Two more reds die despite everything. But one — one red, the woman you marked, the one who watched you chalk her forehead — she survives. Against every probability, her body fights and your treatment is just enough.
Nine alive, nine dead. A worse ratio than triage would have given you. But the woman is alive, and she looks at you from her cot with eyes that know exactly what you sacrificed to keep her here.
Dr. Okafor arrives at dawn. He counts. He sees the ratios. He understands what you chose.
"You can't save everyone," he says.
"I know. But I can refuse to be the one who decides who dies."

choices.text: "Teach me to be better. So I don't have to choose."
"Where do I get real equipment? I need real tools."

jmed_014.title: Okafor's Offer
text: Dr. Okafor takes you to the Sector 7 clinic. In daylight, it is both better and worse than you imagined — better equipped than your shipping container, but still desperately underfunded. The surgical suite is a converted storage room. The pharmacy is a locked cabinet half-empty.
"I've been watching you," Okafor says. "Three weeks, eighteen critical cases, and a chemical spill you handled alone. You have the hands for this. What you need is the knowledge."

He offers you a position. Informal — there are no official medical credentials in the Lower District. But he will teach you. Anatomy, pharmacology, surgical technique. In exchange, you work his clinic's overflow, the cases he can't get to.

"I should warn you," he adds. "The work never stops. The patients never end. And the system that creates them never changes. If you're looking for a career that makes you feel good about the world, this is not it."

choices.text: "I'm not looking to feel good. I'm looking to keep people alive."
"What about the system? Is there a way to change it?"

jmed_015.title: First Lessons
text: Okafor teaches the way he practices — relentlessly, without sentiment, with a precision that borders on the brutal. You learn to set bones by setting them. You learn suturing technique on practice skin until your stitches can hold a millimeter tolerance. You learn anatomy from a battered textbook and from the patients themselves, whose bodies teach lessons no book can.

The clinic's rhythm becomes yours. Morning rounds. Afternoon surgeries. Evening triage. Night emergencies. The sleep you get is stolen in fragments between crises, and your dreams — when you dream — are of wounds that won't close and blood that won't stop.

Three weeks in, Okafor hands you a scalpel and steps back from the table. "This one's yours," he says. The patient is a dockworker with an abscess the size of your fist. You drain it, pack it, close it. Your hands don't shake.

choices.text: Ask Okafor about stack medicine — the real money
Continue focusing on trauma surgery

jmed_016.title: The Stack Question
text: Stack medicine. The two words that divide healthcare in this city like a scalpel divides flesh. Those with cortical stacks get premium care — their consciousness backed up, their bodies treatable as replaceable hardware. Those without are meat. Mortal, vulnerable, disposable meat.

Okafor's expression darkens when you ask. "Stack surgery is controlled by the corporations. Licensed physicians only, registered facilities, full compliance with Meridian Medical Authority protocols. Everything we are not."

He pauses. "But I know things they don't teach in the corporate academies. Things I learned before I lost my license. Things about the stack-body interface that the corporations would prefer stayed buried."
He doesn't elaborate. But you file it away. There are things Okafor knows that he isn't ready to share, and there are reasons he lost his license that he hasn't told you.

choices.text: Don't push. He'll tell you when he's ready
"What happened to your license?"

jmed_017.title: The Scar on Okafor
text: Okafor is quiet for a long time. The clinic hums around you — the soft beep of a heart monitor, the labored breathing of a patient in recovery, the distant sound of rain on metal.

"I was a stack surgeon," he says finally. "Meridian Medical, Level 3 clearance. I performed cortical stack installations for the corporate elite. Backup procedures, consciousness transfers, the whole menu. I was very good at it."

His hands — those steady, precise hands — tremble slightly. "Then I discovered what happens to the failed installations. The stacks that don't integrate properly. The patients whose consciousness fragments during the procedure. The corporations call them 'process losses.' I called them people. I tried to treat them. The corporation called that unauthorized research."

He looks at you directly. "They didn't just take my license. They took my stack. I'm mortal now, same as the people I treat. Everything I know about stack medicine, I carry in my biological brain, which is degrading a little more each year. So yes — I know stack surgery. And yes, someday I will teach you. But not today."

choices.text: "I understand. I'll earn it."
"What happened to those patients? The fragmented ones?"

jmed_018.title: Gang War Casualties
text: The Bone Saints and Iron Chain go to war over a transit corridor. The casualties arrive in waves — first the fighters, then the bystanders, then the children who were in the wrong place when the bullets started flying.

You and Okafor work side by side for sixteen hours. He teaches you while he operates, a running commentary on technique delivered over open wounds: "See this bleeder? Clamp here, not there. There hits the nerve. Here saves the arm."
Between patients, a Bone Saints lieutenant arrives. He doesn't want treatment. He wants information. Which Iron Chain fighters have you treated? Where are they? How badly are they hurt?
The lieutenant puts a credstick on the table. It's worth more than your monthly earnings.

choices.text: "I don't sell patient information. Get out."
"I treat wounds, not sides. Both gangs bleed the same."
Consider it — the money could buy real medical equipment

jmed_019.title: Neutrality Has a Price
text: The lieutenant picks up his credstick and leaves without a word. Okafor watches him go, then looks at you with something that might be approval.

"Good," he says. "Medical neutrality is the only thing keeping us alive in this district. The moment we pick a side, we become a target for the other. And the moment we sell information, we become a tool instead of a healer."

That night, someone breaks into the clinic and steals half your antibiotic supply. A message, or maybe just opportunism — in the Lower District, the distinction rarely matters. Either way, you're short on supplies and the gang war is still producing casualties.
You need to resupply. The question is how.

choices.text: Raid the abandoned Meridian Medical depot in Sector 9
Go to the black market and pay whatever it costs

jmed_020.title: The Compromise
text: You take the credstick. You tell yourself it's for equipment. For supplies. For the patients who will die without better tools. You tell yourself the information you give is limited — just enough to satisfy, not enough to get anyone killed.

The lieutenant nods and leaves. Okafor watches, and says nothing, but his silence is louder than any condemnation. You have crossed a line, and you both know it, and the credits in your hand feel exactly as heavy as you expected.
Two days later, three Iron Chain members are ambushed in a transit corridor. One dies. The other two arrive at your clinic, bleeding, and look at you with eyes that do not know what you did.
You treat them. Of course you treat them. And the guilt settles into a place in your chest where it will live for a very long time.

choices.text: Never again. Use the money for supplies and move forward
The guilt is the price. Buy the equipment

jmed_021.title: The Depot Raid
text: The abandoned Meridian Medical depot sits in the scrap yards, behind a fence that hasn't been maintained in years. The building still has the corporate logo on the door, faded but legible, a reminder that this district once had corporate medical infrastructure before it was deemed unprofitable.

Inside, the depot has been picked over but not emptied. You find expired medications — some still viable, some dangerous, and the knowledge to tell the difference is one of Okafor's gifts. You find surgical tools, dulled but usable. You find an autoclave that might work with some repair. And in a locked cabinet in the back, you find something unexpected: a case of cortical stack interface cables. Military grade. Worth a fortune.
The cables are not medical supplies. They are something else entirely — tools for interfacing with the technology that defines who lives and who dies in this city.

choices.text: Take only the medical supplies. Leave the stack cables
Take everything. The cables might be useful someday

jmed_022.title: Market Medicine
text: The black market's pharmaceutical section has grown since your last visit. Kess has expanded, or at least her inventory has. You find antibiotics, analgesics, surgical thread, sterile dressings, and a portable diagnostic scanner that looks like it fell off a corporate ambulance — which it probably did.
"Business is good when people are dying," Kess says without irony. She is a businesswoman in a business that profits from suffering, and she has made peace with that fact in a way you have not.

You buy what you can afford. The diagnostic scanner alone would transform your practice — real-time blood analysis, organ imaging, the ability to see inside a patient without cutting them open. But the price is steep.
"I can extend credit," Kess offers. "But I charge interest. And I collect."

choices.text: Buy the essentials only — stay out of debt
Take the credit. The scanner will save lives

jmed_023.title: The Child with the Cough
text: A mother brings a child to you at midnight. The child is eight, thin as wire, coughing blood. Not the wet cough of infection but the dry, racking cough of something systemic. Something wrong at a level deeper than lungs.

You examine him carefully. Elevated temperature. Lymph nodes swollen. A rash on his torso in a pattern you recognize from Okafor's textbook — the signature of Kessler's Syndrome, a neurological condition caused by exposure to unshielded stack radiation.
The irony cuts deep. A child too poor to have a cortical stack, sickened by the radiation from other people's stacks. The rich buy immortality; the poor pay for it with their children's health.
There is a treatment. It is expensive, corporate-controlled, and not available in the Lower District. But it exists.

choices.text: Tell the mother the truth and try to source the treatment
Treat the symptoms and buy time while you figure out options

jmed_024.title: The Corporate Wall
text: You take the case to Okafor. He recognizes Kessler's Syndrome immediately — the way his face goes still tells you he has seen it before and the memory is not kind.

"The treatment is called Neurex-7," he says. "Meridian Pharma holds the patent. It costs four thousand credits per dose. The child will need three doses over six weeks. Twelve thousand credits total."
Twelve thousand credits. A number so large it might as well be a galaxy away. The mother earns thirty credits a week at the textile works. The math is impossible.

"There's another option," Okafor says carefully. "I can synthesize an analog. Not as effective, but functional. I need a lab, precursor chemicals, and about a week. The problem is that synthesizing patented pharmaceuticals is a crime that carries a ten-year sentence in the corporate labor camps."
He looks at you. "Your call."

choices.text: "We synthesize it. A child's life is worth the risk."
"Let me try to get the real thing first. There might be a legal way."

jmed_025.title: Breaking the Law to Save a Life
text: The lab is in the tunnels beneath the transit hub — a space Okafor has maintained in secret for years, equipped with chemistry apparatus and a library of pharmaceutical formulas stored on encrypted datapads. He has been synthesizing medications for the Lower District's uninsured for longer than you've been practicing medicine.

The Neurex-7 analog takes six days to produce. You assist, learning pharmaceutical chemistry through the process, watching Okafor work with the precision of a man who once held a corporate lab's full resources and now operates with salvage and determination.

On the seventh day, you administer the first dose. The child's mother holds his hand. You hold your breath. Within hours, his cough lessens. Within a day, the rash begins to fade. It is not a cure — the analog is imperfect, and he will need ongoing treatment — but it is enough. It is a life, continuing.
You are now a criminal. The weight of it should be heavier than it is.

choices.text: You're ready for more. Ask Okafor about running a real clinic
Take time to process what just happened. Rushing forward leads to mistakes

jmed_026.title: The Clinic
text: Okafor's proposal is simple and impossible: take over the Sector 7 clinic's night shift. Full autonomy. Your own patients, your own decisions, your own mistakes. He will be available for consultation but the hands on the scalpel will be yours.

The clinic is four rooms: a waiting area with mismatched chairs, an examination room with a table that wobbles, a surgical suite that would make a corporate doctor weep, and a supply closet that is always emptier than it should be.

Your first night as Clinic Runner, you see eleven patients. A fractured wrist. Two cases of food poisoning from contaminated protein rations. A knife wound that someone claims was an accident and clearly wasn't. An elderly woman whose heart is failing in slow motion, each visit a little worse, each treatment buying a little less time.
Welcome to medicine. Rank 2.

choices.text: Establish a routine and build the clinic's reputation
Build relationships with the community first — the clinic needs trust before patients

jmed_027.title: Supply and Demand
text: Running a clinic is not just medicine. It is logistics, diplomacy, and triage of a different kind — triaging resources instead of patients. You learn to track inventory like a quartermaster, to negotiate with suppliers like a merchant, to manage the expectations of patients who need everything when you have almost nothing.

The clinic receives a monthly supply allocation from a charitable trust — barely enough for two weeks of operation. The rest you scrounge, barter, and occasionally steal. The ethics of theft become flexible when the alternative is watching patients die from treatable conditions.

One week, a shipment arrives with an anomaly: twice the normal supply of morphine, packed in unmarked containers. Either someone made a mistake, or someone is using your clinic as a waypoint for drug distribution.

choices.text: Investigate the morphine shipment quietly
Keep the morphine — patients need pain relief regardless of its origin

jmed_028.title: The Pipeline
text: You trace the morphine shipment through a network of intermediaries that leads, eventually, to a mid-level Bone Saints lieutenant named Perez. The gang has been using charitable medical supply chains to move narcotics through the district, piggybacking on legitimate shipments to avoid patrol scrutiny.

Perez is unapologetic when you confront him. "We keep your clinic open," he says. "Half your supply chain runs through our territory. You think those shipments arrive safely by accident? We provide security. The morphine is our tax."
He's not entirely wrong. The supply corridors through the Lower District are dangerous, and the Bone Saints do patrol them. Without that protection, your shipments would be raided within a week.
"So here's the deal," Perez says. "We keep the pipeline secure. You keep receiving your supplies. And every third shipment carries a little extra cargo that doesn't concern you."

choices.text: Refuse. Find another supply route, even if it's harder
Accept the arrangement. The clinic needs those supplies

jmed_029.title: Don't Ask, Don't Treat
text: You keep the morphine and don't investigate. The patients who need it — and there are many, because pain is the most democratic condition in the Lower District — receive proper pain management for the first time. You watch a man with terminal bone cancer close his eyes in relief as the morphine takes hold, and you think: whatever the source, this is what the drug was made for.

But willful ignorance has its own weight. Each shipment arrives with the same anomaly, and you say nothing, and the silence becomes its own kind of complicity. You are a doctor who does not ask where the medicine comes from, and in the Lower District, that makes you normal.

Three weeks later, a teenager overdoses on morphine from a batch that matches your clinic's stock. He survives, barely. His mother comes to you, not knowing the connection, and asks you to treat him. You do.

choices.text: The overdose changes things. Investigate the source now
Tighten your own inventory controls but don't rock the boat

jmed_030.title: New Routes
text: You cut ties with the compromised supply chain and spend two weeks building alternatives. A sympathetic transit worker helps you route shipments through maintenance tunnels. A retired nurse in the market district connects you with a gray-market medical supplier who deals in surplus, not stolen goods.
The new supply chain is slower, less reliable, and more expensive. But it's clean. No hidden cargo, no gang taxation, no complicity in the drug trade that kills the same people you're trying to heal.

The transition costs you. Three weeks of reduced supplies means rationing. Patients notice. Some seek treatment elsewhere — from unlicensed dealers whose remedies are as likely to harm as heal. The ripple effects of your principles spread outward in ways you can't control.
But the clinic survives. And when you lock up at the end of a shift, your hands are clean in every sense that matters.

onEnter.reason: Cost of establishing new supply routes

choices.text: Focus on building the clinic's capacity with honest means
Explore the black market route carefully — regulated doesn't mean safe

jmed_031.title: The Devil's Bargain
text: You accept the arrangement. The supplies keep flowing. The clinic stays open. The extra cargo passes through your storage room in sealed containers you never open, handled by people who arrive after hours and leave before dawn.

You are saving lives during the day and enabling destruction at night, and the cognitive dissonance becomes a physical sensation — a tightness in your chest that never quite releases. This is what compromise feels like: not a single dramatic betrayal but a slow erosion of the line between what you'll accept and what you won't.
Okafor knows. He doesn't say anything directly, but his silences have a particular quality — the silence of a man who has made similar bargains and regrets every one.
"The clinic is doing good work," he says one evening. What he means is: is the good work enough to offset the rest? You don't have an answer.

onEnter.reason: Reduced supply costs through gang arrangement

choices.text: Keep the arrangement and focus on the patients
Renegotiate the bargain — better terms are possible if you push

jmed_032.title: The Impossible Choice
text: Two patients arrive at the same moment. You have one vial of antivenom — the last in the district.

Patient one: a child, seven years old, bitten by a tunnel spider. Without the antivenom, the venom will shut down her kidneys within hours. She is crying softly, asking for her mother, who is holding her hand with white-knuckled desperation.

Patient two: Malik, a fighter who protects the neighborhood. He was bitten during a patrol of the water treatment facility — the same patrol that keeps the tunnel spiders from reaching the residential blocks. Without the antivenom, he dies. Without his patrols, more people get bitten. More children, potentially.
One vial. Two patients. The mathematics of medicine at its most cruel.

choices.text: Give the antivenom to the child
Give the antivenom to Malik — he protects the whole neighborhood
Try to split the dose between them

jmed_033.title: The Child Lives
text: You give the antivenom to the child. She screams when the needle enters, then quiets as the medicine works, her small body unclenching from the poison's grip. Her kidneys stabilize. Her fever drops. She will live, and she will not remember this night, which is perhaps the kindest thing about childhood.
Malik watches you make the choice. He nods — a single, slow movement. "Good," he says. "That's right."

You fight to save him with everything else you have. Dialysis improvised from spare parts. Blood filtration. Hydration. It is not enough. Malik dies at 4:17 AM, quietly, without complaint, as if dying were simply another duty he performed with professional competence.
The neighborhood feels his absence within days. The tunnel spider population pushes closer to the residential blocks. Two more bites in the first week. You have no antivenom for those either.
You saved a child and lost a protector. The calculus of that equation will never balance.

choices.text: Organize a volunteer patrol to replace Malik's role
Focus on sourcing more antivenom to prevent future dilemmas

jmed_034.title: The Protector Lives
text: You give the antivenom to Malik. Your hands are steady. Your reasoning is sound. He protects dozens of lives. The utilitarian math is clear.
The child's mother does not scream. She does not rage. She holds her daughter and watches you treat the man you chose instead, and her silence is the loudest thing you have ever heard.

You fight for the child with everything remaining. Fluid therapy. Toxin binding agents improvised from activated charcoal. Her kidneys fail at 2 AM. She enters a coma at 3. She dies at dawn, in her mother's arms, and the sound the woman makes is not a scream but something lower and more terrible — a sound that comes from a place where language has not yet been invented because no word could hold this weight.
Malik recovers. He returns to patrol. The spider population stays in check. More children are safe because of him. None of those children are the one who died on your table.
The mother never comes to the clinic again.

choices.text: Carry the weight and keep working
Document the case thoroughly. Others will face this same situation

jmed_035.title: The Half Measure
text: You split the dose. It is medically inadvisable, potentially fatal to both patients, and the only choice you can live with. Okafor would tell you it's wrong. The textbooks would tell you it's wrong. But the textbooks weren't written for a world where one vial is all that stands between two people and death.

The half-doses slow the venom in both patients. You buy time — hours instead of the minutes you had before. You use those hours to fight, deploying every technique Okafor taught you and some you invent on the spot.

The child stabilizes first. Her younger body processes the reduced dose more efficiently. Malik takes longer, his larger frame struggling with the insufficient antivenom, but his physical resilience tips the balance. By dawn, both are alive. Barely. Kidney function compromised in both. Long recovery ahead for both.
Both alive. Both damaged. Both looking at you with gratitude you don't feel you've earned.

choices.text: Monitor both patients through their recovery
Review what went wrong. Half measures shouldn't be the standard

jmed_036.title: The Plague Begins
text: It starts with a cough. It always starts with a cough. Three patients in one day, all from Block 22, all with the same symptoms: dry cough, fever, joint pain, and a distinctive blue-tinged rash that spreads from the chest outward like a map of capillaries drawn in bruise.

You've never seen anything like it. Neither has Okafor. He examines the rash under magnification and goes very quiet — the particular silence of a man encountering something that frightens him, which frightens you in turn because you have never seen Okafor afraid.
"Isolate them," he says. "Now. Separate room, sealed door, masks for everyone who enters. And send someone to Block 22. I need to know how many more there are."
There are seventeen more. All in the same residential stack. All with the same symptoms. All deteriorating.

choices.text: Set up a quarantine zone in the clinic
Go to Block 22 personally to assess the situation

jmed_037.title: Block 22
text: Block 22 is a vertical stack of forty residential containers, each housing a family or two or three. The ventilation system is shared — one set of ducts serving all forty units, recycling the same air through the same filters that haven't been changed in months.

You go door to door. The sick are easy to find — the cough echoes in the metal corridors. The healthy watch you with the particular wariness of people who know that a doctor going door to door means something terrible is happening.
The pattern emerges: the sickest patients live closest to the central ventilation shaft. Whatever this is, it's airborne, and it's been circulating through Block 22's shared air supply for days.

You take samples — sputum, blood, swabs of the ventilation ducts. Okafor's lab equipment is limited but functional. What you find makes your stomach drop: a synthetic pathogen. Engineered. Someone made this.

choices.text: Tell Okafor immediately — this needs to be reported
Investigate the ventilation system for the source

jmed_038.title: Engineered Suffering
text: Okafor examines your samples and confirms what you feared. The pathogen is synthetic — a modified respiratory virus with engineered surface proteins. Someone designed this. Someone built it in a lab and introduced it into Block 22's ventilation system.
"Who would do this?" you ask, though you already know the answer isn't a person but a category: someone who benefits from the Lower District's suffering.

"The question isn't who," Okafor says. "The question is why Block 22 specifically. What makes that block special?" He pauses. "Block 22 is the largest concentration of unregistered residents in the sector. No stacks, no ID, no official existence. If they all die, there's no record they ever lived."
The implications settle over you like a weight. A targeted bioweapon, aimed at people who don't exist in the system. The perfect crime: you can't murder people who were never recorded as alive.
The death toll stands at four. It will rise.

choices.text: Focus on treating the sick while Okafor works on a cure
Try to find the source — who planted the pathogen?

jmed_039.title: The Race
text: You split responsibilities. Okafor works on an antiviral while you run the quarantine ward. The clinic transforms into an emergency hospital, cots lining every wall, patients coughing the distinctive dry cough that has become the soundtrack of your nightmares.

The death toll rises: seven, then twelve, then nineteen. Children and elderly first, as always. The strong survive longer but deteriorate steadily, the blue rash spreading across their bodies like a countdown.

On the fourth day, Okafor emerges from his lab with bloodshot eyes and a vial of clear liquid. "I have something," he says. "It's untested. It might work. It might accelerate the pathogen. There's no time for trials."
He holds up the vial. "We need to decide. Test it on the next patient who's going to die anyway, or distribute it now and hope."

choices.text: Test it first — even in crisis, you need to know it works
Distribute it now — people are dying every hour

jmed_040.title: The Test
text: You test the antiviral on a patient named Reena, sixty-three, who is dying. You tell her what you're doing and what the risks are. She listens with the calm attention of a woman who has lived long enough to accept uncertainty.
"Try it," she says. "If it works, give it to the others. If it kills me, I was dying anyway and at least I helped."

You administer the dose. For six hours, nothing changes. Then Reena's fever drops. The cough lessens. The blue rash begins to recede from her extremities, retreating toward her chest like a tide going out.

Twelve hours later, Reena is sitting up and asking for soup. The antiviral works. Three more patients died during the test period — three lives that the delay cost. But now you know the treatment is safe, and you begin mass distribution with confidence.

choices.text: Begin treating all patients immediately
Triage ruthlessly — treat the most critical first, not everyone at once

jmed_041.title: The Gamble
text: You distribute immediately. No testing, no trials, no safety margin. Every patient in the quarantine ward receives a dose, and you spend the next six hours watching them with the particular anxiety of someone who has just rolled dice with thirty lives.

The gamble pays off. Within twelve hours, the fevers drop. The coughs lessen. The blue rashes begin to fade. Not everyone recovers — four patients were too far gone, their organs too damaged by the pathogen to respond — but the rest improve steadily.
Okafor watches the recovery with an expression that mixes relief with something darker. "We got lucky," he says. "If the antiviral had been toxic, we would have killed everyone in this room."
"But it wasn't, and we didn't."
"No. This time."

choices.text: "Sometimes luck is all you've got down here."
"Luck and preparation. We need more of the second."

jmed_042.title: Aftermath
text: The plague is contained. Twenty-seven dead out of over a hundred infected — a number that would be considered a miracle in the corporate medical system and a catastrophe in any just world. You write each name in a ledger that Okafor keeps, a record of every patient lost, because if the system won't acknowledge these people existed, at least their doctor will.

In the wake of the crisis, the clinic's reputation transforms. You are no longer just a street doc. You are the one who fought the plague. People come from blocks away, from districts you've never visited. The waiting room overflows.
But the question of the engineered pathogen remains unanswered. Someone made that virus. Someone deployed it. And if they did it once, they can do it again.

choices.text: Investigate the pathogen's origin — who made it and why?
Focus on preparedness — stockpile antivirals for the next outbreak

jmed_043.title: Following the Trail
text: The investigation leads you to the Data Haven, where a contact traces the pathogen's genetic signature to a Meridian BioTech subsidiary called ClearPoint Solutions. A company that officially produces agricultural pest control agents and unofficially produces whatever its corporate masters require.

The evidence is circumstantial but damning: ClearPoint held a patent on the exact protein structure found in the Block 22 pathogen. The patent was filed three months before the outbreak and marked 'classified — internal use only.'
You now have information that could expose a corporation for bioterrorism against unregistered citizens. Information that could save future lives or end yours, depending on what you do with it.
Okafor's advice: "Be careful. Corporations don't commit crimes — they commit policies. And they eliminate policy risks."

choices.text: Store the evidence safely. It may be useful later
Try to leak it to an independent journalist

jmed_044.title: Preparing for the Next One
text: You focus on preparedness. Okafor teaches you to synthesize the antiviral in larger batches, and you build a stockpile in the clinic's secure storage. You establish quarantine protocols, train volunteer responders, create an early-warning network through the district's informal communication channels.

The work is unglamorous and essential — the kind of medicine that prevents disasters rather than heroically responding to them. You spend weeks on logistics instead of surgery, and the shift feels wrong until a water contamination event in Block 15 triggers your protocols and the response you've built catches it before it becomes an epidemic.

Zero deaths. The absence of a crisis is the hardest victory to celebrate because there's nothing to point to, no dramatic moment, just the quiet continuation of lives that would have ended without your preparation.
Okafor nods approvingly. "Prevention is the most thankless form of medicine. Nobody thanks you for the epidemic that didn't happen."

choices.text: You've proven yourself. Ask Okafor about the next level
Take a rest day first. Burnout kills doctors slower but just as dead

jmed_045.title: Clinic Politics
text: The clinic's growth brings complications. A community council forms to oversee operations, and they have opinions about everything — staffing, supply allocation, treatment priorities. A faction wants to reserve a portion of the clinic's resources for paying patients to generate revenue. Another faction wants everything free, funded by donations that are never quite sufficient.
You sit in a meeting and listen to people argue about the price of antibiotics while outside the door, a man with a broken arm waits on a chair that's missing a leg.

"We need to be sustainable," says Councillor Voss, a retired accountant who speaks in spreadsheets. "Free medicine is beautiful in theory. In practice, it's a clinic that closes in six months when the money runs out."
"And paid medicine is a clinic that turns away the people who need it most," replies Sister Dena, who runs the food kitchen next door. "We might as well be the corporations."

choices.text: Side with Voss — sustainability matters, even if it means some patients pay
Side with Sister Dena — free care is the whole point
Propose a compromise — sliding scale based on ability to pay

jmed_046.title: An Old Enemy Returns
text: A corporate medical inspector appears at the clinic's door. Clean suit, datapad, the faintly predatory smile of someone who enforces regulations for a living. He introduces himself as Inspector Graves and says he's conducting a routine compliance review.

There is nothing routine about it. The clinic operates without a license, dispenses medication without authorization, and employs a doctor — you — who has no recognized credentials. Every aspect of your operation violates corporate medical law.
"I can shut this clinic down today," Graves says pleasantly. "Or we can discuss alternatives."

The alternatives, it becomes clear, involve the clinic registering with the Meridian Medical Authority, accepting corporate oversight, and — crucially — requiring stack registration for all patients. The very thing the clinic was built to avoid.

choices.text: Stall him while you figure out options
Tell him to do his worst — you won't register patients

jmed_047.title: Buying Time
text: You stall Graves with paperwork requests, procedural objections, and a tour of the clinic that you stretch to maximum duration. While he inspects the surgical suite with undisguised disdain, Okafor makes calls.

By the time Graves leaves — with a 30-day compliance deadline — Okafor has found leverage. "Graves reports to the Meridian Medical Authority. The Authority reports to the corporate board. And the corporate board just received an anonymous data packet about ClearPoint Solutions and the Block 22 pathogen."
"You had the evidence?" you ask.

"I had copies. I always keep copies." Okafor looks tired. "This buys us time. Not absolution. They'll come back. They always come back. But right now, they're more worried about the bioweapon scandal than an unlicensed clinic."
The 30-day deadline passes without enforcement.

choices.text: Use the breathing room to strengthen the clinic's position
Expand the clinic's services while you have room to breathe

jmed_048.title: Defiance
text: You tell Graves to leave. Not politely. The clinic exists because the corporate medical system failed these people, and you will not invite that system back in through the front door.

Graves smiles the smile of a man who expected this answer and prepared for it. "You have seven days," he says. "After that, enforcement officers will close this facility and arrest everyone inside it."

Seven days. You spend them mobilizing. The community rallies — patients, families, neighbors who have never set foot in the clinic but understand what its loss would mean. When the enforcement officers arrive, they find three hundred people blocking the clinic's entrance. Not armed, not violent, just present. A wall of bodies that says: this is ours.
The officers withdraw. Not because of compassion but because of optics — clearing a medical clinic by force would generate attention that the corporations prefer to avoid. For now.
You've won a battle. The war continues.

choices.text: Prepare for whatever comes next
Build alliances with other clinics. Defiance works better in numbers

jmed_049.title: A Mentor's Gift
text: Okafor is dying. He tells you on a Tuesday afternoon, between patients, as if it were a clinical observation rather than a life sentence. The neurological degradation from his stack removal has progressed to the point where his fine motor control is compromised. His hands — those legendary hands — tremble now even at rest.

"I have perhaps six months of useful function left," he says. "After that, I will not be able to operate. After that, I will not be able to think clearly. After that..." He trails off. The prognosis doesn't need completion.

"I need to teach you everything I know before I can't. Stack surgery. The procedures the corporations don't want anyone outside their system to perform. The knowledge that got me exiled to this clinic. Are you ready?"
The weight of what he's offering settles on you. Not just knowledge — responsibility. Legacy. The burden of being the only one who knows.

choices.text: "Teach me everything. I'll carry what you can't."
"I'll carry what I can. Some things you need to write down instead."

jmed_050.title: Forbidden Knowledge
text: Okafor takes you into the tunnels, deeper than you've been before, to a second lab you didn't know existed. This one is different from the pharmaceutical lab — it is a surgical theater, hidden, equipped with technology that should not exist outside a corporate hospital. A stack interface station. Neural mapping equipment. Tools for the most intimate form of surgery: opening the barrier between body and soul.

"Stack surgery," Okafor says, "is not about the hardware. The cortical stack is a piece of technology. Any competent surgeon can install one. What makes stack surgery sacred — and what the corporations will never admit — is that you are touching consciousness itself. The stack doesn't just record memories. It records identity. Continuity of self. Everything that makes a person a person."
He picks up a cortical stack from the table. It's the size of a thumbnail. "This holds someone's soul. Treat it accordingly."
Rank 3. Stack Surgeon.

choices.text: Begin learning stack surgery from Okafor
Question the ethics first. Forbidden knowledge is forbidden for reasons

jmed_051.title: The First Stack
text: Okafor begins with theory. Days of it. The neuroanatomy of the stack-brain interface. The protocols for consciousness transfer. The failure modes — and there are many — that can shatter a mind during installation.

"The stack integrates with the brain stem at the C1 vertebra," he explains, his trembling hands holding a holographic diagram steady through force of will. "The neural filaments extend into the hippocampus, the prefrontal cortex, the amygdala. They map everything. But the mapping process is traumatic. The brain doesn't want to be read. It resists."
"What happens when it resists?"

"Fragmentation. The consciousness splits. Parts of the personality encode to the stack while other parts remain in the organic brain. The patient becomes two incomplete people sharing one body. The corporations call it 'integration failure.' I call it what it is: a broken soul."

choices.text: "How do you prevent fragmentation?"
"Can the fragmented be healed?"

jmed_052.title: Practice on the Dead
text: You practice on cadavers first. Okafor obtains them from the district's unclaimed dead — people who died without family, without stack, without anyone to claim them. You feel the weight of using the dead to learn, but the alternative is learning on the living, and that weight is heavier.

The surgical technique is extraordinarily delicate. The stack implant must be positioned within a millimeter of the optimal neural junction. The filament deployment must be controlled precisely — too fast and the filaments tear brain tissue; too slow and the neural pathway mapping fails.

Your first attempt on a cadaver takes four hours and fails. The second takes three hours and partially succeeds. The fifth takes ninety minutes and succeeds cleanly. Okafor watches with his trembling hands folded in his lap, teaching through observation and correction, his voice steady even when his body is not.
"You have the hands for this," he says. "Better than mine, now. Better than they were, even before."

choices.text: "I need a living patient to truly learn."
Continue practicing until you're certain of your skill

jmed_053.title: The First Living Patient
text: The patient is a woman named Sera, thirty-four, a transit worker who lost her corporate stack registration when her employer went bankrupt. Without a registered stack, she cannot access the transit systems that are her livelihood. She needs a new stack installed — an unregistered one, illegal, but functional.

She sits on the surgical table with the calm of someone who has weighed the risks and made her peace. "I need to work," she says simply. "Without the stack, I don't exist in the system. Without existing in the system, I can't feed my kids."

Okafor guides you through the procedure, his voice low and steady in your ear. The incision. The exposure of the C1 junction. The precise placement of the stack. The nerve-wracking moment when you deploy the filaments and Sera's body goes rigid with the shock of neural integration.
Three minutes of absolute terror. Then her body relaxes. Her eyes open. She looks at you.
"Did it work?"
You run the diagnostic. Clean integration. Zero fragmentation. A perfect installation.

choices.text: "It worked. You're going to be fine."
Run full diagnostics before celebrating. Early success hides late complications

jmed_054.title: The Weight of Immortality
text: Word spreads in the way that dangerous knowledge always does — through whispers, through need, through the desperate networks of people who have no other options. You are a stack surgeon. An unlicensed, underground stack surgeon who can give people what the corporations gatekeep: functional immortality.

The implications settle over you in layers. With a cortical stack, consciousness can be backed up, transferred, restored after death. Without one, death is permanent. You are now the person who decides who gets to live forever and who doesn't.

The requests come daily. People who need stacks for work. People who want stacks for safety. People who are dying and want their consciousness preserved. Each one is a risk — illegal surgery, performed in a hidden lab, with consequences that could destroy everything you've built.

Okafor watches your struggle with the knowing eyes of a man who has fought this same battle. "The power to grant immortality is the heaviest thing a human being can hold," he says. "Don't let it crush you."

choices.text: Limit stack surgeries to those who truly need them for survival
Anyone who wants a stack deserves access — expand operations

jmed_055.title: The Gatekeeper
text: You become the thing you swore you wouldn't: a gatekeeper. You decide who gets stack surgery based on need — workers who can't function without stack registration, people with terminal conditions whose consciousness deserves preservation, children whose futures depend on being part of the system.

The ones you turn away don't always understand. A man who wants a stack for his elderly mother argues with you for an hour. "She's going to die," he says. "You can save her. Not her body — her. Everything she is. And you're saying no?"

You're saying not yet. Not today. Not with the limited supplies and the risk of discovery and the reality that every surgery you perform is a crime that could shut down the clinic and leave everyone — stacked and unstacked — without medical care.
The man leaves angry. His mother dies two weeks later, mortal and final. He never comes back.

choices.text: Hold the line. The clinic's survival matters more than any individual case
Find a compromise. Gatekeeping and open doors both have costs

jmed_056.title: Open Access
text: You expand. More surgeries, more patients, more stacks flowing through the underground lab. You recruit assistants — a former corporate nurse named Lin who lost her position for whistleblowing, a self-taught tech named Ravi who can configure stack hardware in his sleep.

The operation grows beyond what the hidden lab can support. You set up a second surgical suite in the tunnels, then a third in a converted basement in the old quarter. Each site increases your capacity and your risk.
For three months, it works. Forty-seven successful installations. Zero fragmentation. Zero deaths. You are running the Lower District's only stack surgery practice, and you are good at it.
Then a patient's unregistered stack pings a corporate scanner during a routine transit check. The signal traces back to your lab's hardware signature. Someone is coming.

choices.text: Shut everything down and go dark
Change the hardware signatures and keep operating

jmed_057.title: The Death on Your Table
text: It happens on a Thursday. The patient is a young man named Kenji, twenty-two, who needs a stack replacement — his original was damaged in an accident, and without replacement, his backed-up consciousness will degrade within weeks.

The surgery begins normally. Incision, exposure, extraction of the damaged stack. You've done this before. But when you begin installing the replacement, something goes wrong. The neural filaments deploy unevenly. Kenji's body seizes. The integration monitor screams a warning: fragmentation detected.

You fight to stabilize him. Every technique Okafor taught you, every improvisation you've learned, every ounce of skill in your hands. For forty-seven minutes, you battle the fragmentation cascade, trying to remap the neural pathways before the damage becomes permanent.
At minute forty-eight, Kenji's heart stops. You start CPR. You shock him. You pump him full of stimulants. His heart restarts, stops again, restarts.

At minute sixty-three, you stop. Kenji is gone. The stack recorded partial consciousness data — fragments of a person, echoes without enough continuity to reconstitute. He is dead in every way that matters.

choices.text: Sit with the body. You owe him that
Analyze what went wrong. Prevent it from happening again

jmed_058.title: After
text: You tell Kenji's family. His sister receives the news with a stillness that you recognize — the stillness of a person who is processing something too large to process, who is shelving the grief for later because the present moment requires composure.
"Could anyone have saved him?" she asks.

"I don't know." It is the hardest honest answer you've ever given. Because you don't know. Maybe a corporate surgeon with better equipment could have managed the fragmentation. Maybe no one could have. The uncertainty is a wound that won't close.
Okafor finds you in the lab afterward, staring at the surgical table where Kenji died. His trembling hand settles on your shoulder.
"Every surgeon loses patients," he says. "The ones who stop feeling it are the ones you should worry about."
"I can still feel it."
"Good. Use it. Let it make you more careful, not more afraid."

choices.text: Review every aspect of the procedure for improvements
Take time away from stack surgery to recover

jmed_059.title: The Fragmented Ones
text: In the aftermath of Kenji's death, you discover something Okafor has been hiding. In a sealed room behind the lab, there are patients — three of them — in medically induced comas. Their cortical stacks blink with irregular patterns. Their faces twitch with expressions that belong to different people.

"The fragmented ones," Okafor says. "From my time at Meridian. I couldn't save them, but I couldn't let the corporation dispose of them either. So I took them when I left. I've been maintaining them for seven years."

Seven years. Three patients in comas, their consciousness shattered between stack and brain, neither alive nor dead in any meaningful sense. Okafor has been feeding them, hydrating them, monitoring their vitals, keeping their bodies functional while their minds drift in fragments.
"Can they be healed?" you ask.
"I don't know. I've never had the skill or the equipment to try. But you might."

choices.text: Study the fragmented patients — try to find a way to heal them
"We should let them go, Okafor. This isn't living."

jmed_060.title: Rebuilding Souls
text: You spend weeks studying the fragmented patients. Each one presents a different pattern of damage: one has consciousness split cleanly between stack and brain, another has scrambled memories bleeding between both, the third is the most tragic — her stack contains her personality while her brain contains her memories, creating two half-people who cannot function independently.

You develop a theory. The stack-brain interface is bidirectional. If the original integration scattered the consciousness, perhaps a controlled re-integration could gather it back. Like defragmenting a hard drive, but the drive is a human soul.

The procedure would be unprecedented. It would require you to operate on the stack and the brain simultaneously, remapping neural pathways while realigning stack data. One mistake and you destroy whatever fragments remain.
Okafor cannot help. His hands are too far gone. This is yours alone.

choices.text: Attempt the reintegration on the most stable patient first
Continue studying — you need more data before attempting this

jmed_061.title: The Mercy Question
text: Okafor's face crumbles. Not anger — something deeper. The expression of a man being asked to give up the last thing he has held onto.
"They are people," he says. "Damaged people, but people. Their consciousness still exists, scattered between stack and brain. Ending their life support means ending them. Both halves. Forever."
"Is this living?" you ask, gesturing at the comatose bodies with their twitching faces and blinking stacks. "Seven years of medically maintained unconsciousness, existing as fragments?"
The silence between you fills with the soft beeping of heart monitors and the hum of life support equipment that keeps three broken people suspended between existence and nothing.
"No," Okafor admits. "It isn't living. But it isn't nothing either. And while they exist, there's a chance. Someone, someday, might have the knowledge to make them whole."
He looks at you. "Someone like you."

choices.text: "Then I'll try. I'll try to heal them before it's too late."
"I respect your hope. Let me study them first."

jmed_062.title: The Reintegration
text: You choose the most stable patient: a man named Dmitri, whose consciousness is split cleanly between stack and brain. In theory, a clean split should be the easiest to repair — two complete halves that need to be joined rather than dozens of scrambled fragments that need to be sorted.

The procedure takes eleven hours. You work with a precision that transcends training, guided by an intuition born from every patient you've treated, every wound you've closed, every life you've saved and lost. The neural remapping is like composing music you've never heard — finding harmonies in the data, building bridges between the two halves of a shattered mind.

At hour nine, Dmitri's vital signs destabilize. His brain resists the reintegration, the organic tissue rebelling against the artificial reconnection. You adjust, adapt, find a pathway the brain will accept.
At hour eleven, the integration monitor reads: COHERENCE 94%. Not perfect. But functional. Human.

choices.text: Wake him up
Run more tests first. Waking a fragmented mind prematurely causes damage

jmed_063.title: Dmitri Wakes
text: Dmitri opens his eyes. Seven years of fragmentation, and the first thing he does is blink — the universal human gesture of confusion, of returning to a world that has moved on without you.
"Where..." His voice is a rasp. Seven years of disuse. "Where am I?"
"You're safe," you tell him. "You've been sick for a long time. You're getting better."

The full explanation comes later, in pieces. Dmitri absorbs it with the numb acceptance of a man who has lost seven years and knows that no amount of anger or grief will restore them. He asks about his family. They moved. They were told he was dead. They grieved and continued and built new lives on the foundation of that grief.

"I remember everything," he says. "Both halves. Like being two cameras pointed at the same life from different angles. I remember being me, and I remember being the part of me that wasn't quite me. It was..." He trails off. There are no words for what it was.
Okafor weeps. The first time you've seen him cry.

choices.text: Attempt reintegration on the other two patients
Give Dmitri time to recover before attempting more procedures

jmed_064.title: The Corporate Interest
text: News of Dmitri's recovery reaches ears you'd rather it hadn't. Meridian Medical sends a representative — not Inspector Graves this time, but someone higher: Dr. Elaine Voss, Director of Neural Research, a woman whose published papers on stack integration are the foundational texts of the field.
She arrives at the clinic without bodyguards, without threats, without the corporate mask. She looks tired.
"You reintegrated a fragmented consciousness," she says. "That is something my entire department, with unlimited funding and the best equipment in the city, has never achieved. I need to know how."

The request is simple. The implications are not. If you share the technique, Meridian gains the ability to heal fragmentation — but also to understand consciousness at a level that could be used for manipulation, control, weaponization of the very technology that defines personhood.

choices.text: Share the technique — the knowledge could save thousands of fragmented patients
Refuse — you don't trust what Meridian will do with this knowledge

jmed_065.title: Knowledge Shared
text: You share the technique with Dr. Voss. Not all of it — you hold back the intuitive elements, the adjustments you made by feel rather than formula — but enough for a competent surgeon to replicate the procedure. In exchange, Voss provides equipment: a state-of-the-art neural mapping suite that transforms your underground lab from a miracle of improvisation into a functional surgical theater.
The exchange feels like progress. It also feels like giving a weapon to someone and hoping they point it in the right direction.

Within weeks, Meridian publishes a paper on consciousness reintegration. Your name is nowhere in it. The technique is attributed to "a breakthrough in Meridian Neural Research's ongoing program." Your work, stolen and stamped with a corporate logo.

But the fragmented patients across the city begin receiving treatment. Forty-seven reintegrations in the first month. Lives restored. The knowledge is doing good, even if the credit went to the wrong people.

choices.text: The results matter more than the credit. Keep working
Take credit where due. Anonymity doesn't inspire the next generation

jmed_066.title: Knowledge Guarded
text: You refuse Dr. Voss. Politely but completely. The technique stays with you, in your hands and your memory, nowhere else.

Voss takes the refusal with the composure of a woman accustomed to obstacles. "I understand your concerns," she says. "But consider this: there are over three hundred fragmented patients in Meridian's care facilities alone. You are the only person who can help them. Are you comfortable with that monopoly?"

It's a fair point, and it cuts deep. Three hundred people, trapped between existence and void, waiting for help that only you can provide. And you're refusing to scale that help because you don't trust the institution that would scale it.
"I'll treat them," you say. "Bring them to me. But the technique stays mine."
Voss nods slowly. "That's a lot of surgery for one person. I hope your hands hold out longer than Dr. Okafor's did."

choices.text: Begin treating fragmented patients from Meridian's facilities
Share the knowledge selectively — only with doctors you trust

jmed_067.title: Okafor's Last Day
text: Okafor collapses in the clinic on a morning like any other. One moment he is reviewing patient charts with his trembling hands; the next he is on the floor, his body seizing, his eyes rolling back. The neural degradation has reached his motor cortex.

You stabilize him. You've stabilized hundreds of patients. But this one — this one makes your hands want to shake in a way they haven't since your first day as a street doc. This is your teacher. Your mentor. The man who saw a back-alley surgeon and made a physician.
When the seizure passes, Okafor looks up at you from the clinic cot with clear, exhausted eyes.
"It's time," he says. "I can feel the degradation reaching my cognition. Another week, maybe two, and I won't be myself anymore. I need you to do something for me."
"Anything."
"Save me. Or let me go. But don't let me become something less than who I was."

choices.text: "I can install a stack. Back up your consciousness before the degradation takes everything."
"What do you want, Okafor? Tell me what you want."

jmed_068.title: The Student Operates on the Teacher
text: You install a cortical stack in the man who taught you how. The irony is not lost on either of you. Okafor lies on the table where he once guided your hands, and now your hands guide themselves.

The procedure is flawless. Every lesson, every correction, every piece of knowledge Okafor poured into you manifests in the precision of your work. The stack integrates cleanly. The neural filaments deploy without resistance. Okafor's consciousness — his memories, his knowledge, his identity — encodes to the stack in a cascade of data that represents a life of extraordinary service.
When he wakes, he tests himself: reciting pharmaceutical formulas, reviewing surgical procedures, remembering the names of patients from twenty years ago.
"It's all there," he says, and the relief in his voice is the most human sound you've ever heard. "Even the things I'd rather forget."
His body will still deteriorate. But Okafor — the essential, irreducible Okafor — is backed up. Safe. Immortal in the way that only a stack can provide.

choices.text: "Now teach me everything you have left. While you still can."
"Rest now. The teaching can wait until you're stronger."

jmed_069.title: Okafor's Choice
text: Okafor is quiet for a long time. The clinic's sounds fill the silence — heart monitors, quiet breathing, the distant hum of the ventilation system.

"They took my stack once," he says. "It was the most violent thing anyone has ever done to me. Not the physical procedure — the knowledge that someone could reach inside my skull and take my self. My continuity. Everything I am, ripped away."

He looks at you. "I don't want another stack. I don't want to be backed up. I want to be mortal, the way humans were before we invented ways to cheat death. I want my death to be final. Complete. Mine."
The request hits you like a physical blow. You can save him. You have the skill, the equipment, the knowledge. And he is asking you not to.
"Promise me," he says. "When the time comes, let me go."

choices.text: "I promise." And mean it
"I can't promise that. But I hear you."

jmed_070.title: The Succession
text: Okafor's final weeks as a functioning physician are spent in transfer — not of consciousness, but of authority. He introduces you to the clinic's network of contacts: suppliers, allies, sympathizers in the corporate medical system who leak equipment and information. He transfers the clinic's administrative records, such as they are. He writes letters to colleagues in other districts, recommending you in terms that make you uncomfortable with their generosity.
"You are a better surgeon than I ever was," he writes, and you want to argue but you know that argument would dishonor the honesty with which he says it.

On his last day of practice, Okafor sees one final patient: the foundry worker from your very first case, Tomasz, who comes in for a follow-up. The fishing-line scar on his abdomen has faded to a thin white line. He is alive and healthy and he shakes Okafor's trembling hand without knowing that the trembling is the beginning of the end.
"Take care of them," Okafor tells you that evening. He doesn't specify who. He means everyone.

choices.text: Accept the mantle. You are the clinic's doctor now
Share the burden. Appoint a co-director to prevent single points of failure

jmed_071.title: Chief of Medicine
text: The title is informal, self-appointed, and absurd. Chief of Medicine for a district that the official medical system has abandoned. But the community uses it without irony, because in the absence of legitimate authority, the person who does the work becomes the authority.

The clinic has grown. Three examination rooms, a proper surgical suite, a pharmacy that is almost adequately stocked. You have staff now — Lin the nurse, Ravi the tech, two junior medics you've trained yourself. The patient load is triple what it was when you started. The waiting room is never empty.

And the question that has haunted you since your first night as a street doc in Block 14 has grown from personal to institutional: what kind of medicine will you practice? What kind of healthcare system will you build? In a world that has decided these people are not worth healing, what is the alternative?
Rank 4. Chief of Medicine. The hardest rank of all, because now the choices are systemic.

choices.text: Assess the district's healthcare needs and plan your approach
Focus on training more doctors first. Infrastructure follows personnel

jmed_072.title: The Plague Returns
text: The synthetic plague returns. Not the same strain — evolved, adapted, resistant to the antiviral you developed. It hits Block 15 first, then spreads to Blocks 16 through 20. Within a week, three hundred people are sick. Within two weeks, forty are dead.

This time, you have infrastructure. Quarantine protocols activate. Volunteer responders mobilize. The clinic shifts to emergency mode with practiced efficiency. But the new strain is faster, more virulent, and your existing antiviral barely slows it down.

Okafor, confined to his quarters with barely enough motor function to feed himself, sends a message through Lin: "Check the protein structure. If it's evolved naturally, the mutations will be random. If it's engineered again, they'll be systematic."
You check. The mutations are systematic. Someone has released a second-generation bioweapon. And this time, they've designed it to resist your cure.

choices.text: Develop a new antiviral — you've done it before
Find whoever is releasing these bioweapons and stop them at the source

jmed_073.title: The Cure
text: You work in Okafor's pharmaceutical lab, alone now because his hands can no longer assist. The pathogen is sophisticated — engineered with countermeasures against the exact approach you used last time. Whoever designed it studied your previous cure and built immunity to it.

You need a new approach. You study the pathogen's structure, looking for weaknesses the designer didn't anticipate. Seventy-two hours without sleep. The death toll outside climbs. Sixty. Seventy-five. Each number is a name you will write in Okafor's ledger.

On the third day, you find it. Not a chemical vulnerability but a structural one — the pathogen's engineered proteins have a resonance frequency. A specific electromagnetic pulse can disrupt the protein structure and neutralize the virus. It's not a drug. It's a frequency.
You build a crude EM emitter from salvaged parts. You test it on a blood sample. The pathogen dissolves. You scale the emitter to room size. Then building size.

choices.text: Deploy the cure across the infected blocks
Test the cure more thoroughly before mass deployment. Haste kills

jmed_074.title: Hunting the Source
text: You follow the trail from the first outbreak. ClearPoint Solutions, the Meridian subsidiary. But ClearPoint has been dissolved — officially shut down in the wake of the first plague. Its researchers have been reassigned. Its facilities have been decommissioned.

Except they haven't. Through the Data Haven's information brokers, you discover that ClearPoint's lead researcher — Dr. Marcus Hale — has continued his work in an unofficial lab funded by a faction within the Meridian board. The plague isn't a corporate policy. It's a rogue operation, run by people who believe that the Lower District's unregistered population is a problem that can be solved through biological erasure.

You find Hale's lab in an abandoned water treatment facility in Sector 9. It is exactly what you expected and worse: vials of engineered pathogens, research notes on targeted genetic markers, a schedule of planned releases. Block 22 was the first. Block 15 is the second. There are six more planned.
Hale is not here. But his work is.

choices.text: Destroy the lab and everything in it
Preserve the evidence — this needs to be exposed publicly

jmed_075.title: The Reckoning
text: The second plague is contained. The death toll: eighty-nine. Eighty-nine names in the ledger. Eighty-nine people who died because someone decided they shouldn't exist.

The community looks to you. Not just for healing now, but for direction. You are the Chief of Medicine, and the title has grown beyond the clinical — you are a leader in a district that has been under biological attack, and the question of how to respond defines the future.

You stand in the clinic, surrounded by recovering patients and exhausted staff, and you see four paths forward. Each one shapes the district's healthcare system — and the district itself — in fundamentally different ways.
The weight of the choice is immense. But you've been carrying weights since your first night as a street doc, and your back hasn't broken yet.

choices.text: Build a free healthcare system for the entire Lower District
Use the cure as leverage to force corporate reform
Expose everything publicly — the bioweapons, the corporate crimes, all of it
Go after Hale directly — some problems require personal solutions

jmed_076.title: The People's Hospital — Part 1
text: You choose to build. Not just a clinic — a system. A healthcare network that spans the Lower District, funded by the community, staffed by trained volunteers, equipped with synthesized medicines and salvaged technology.
