A system that doesn't depend on corporate goodwill or gang protection or the black market.

The task is enormous. You need facilities, equipment, trained personnel, supply chains, funding, and the political will to sustain it all against the inevitable corporate pushback. But you have something the corporations don't: a community that has seen what you can do and believes in what you're building.

You start with satellite clinics — small outposts in each block, staffed by the medics you've trained, connected to the central clinic by a runner network. Basic care at the block level, advanced care at the center. A hub-and-spoke model built on shipping containers and determination.

choices.text: Continue building the network
Slow down. Network quality matters more than speed

jmed_077.title: The People's Hospital — Part 2
text: The network grows. Twelve satellite clinics in the first month. A pharmacy that synthesizes generic medications. A training program that produces competent field medics in six weeks. An emergency response system that can reach any point in the district within fifteen minutes.

The corporations notice. They cannot allow an independent healthcare system to succeed in the Lower District because success would prove that their system — expensive, exclusive, stratified by stack status — is not the only option. If the Lower District can build its own medicine, what else can it build on its own?

The pressure comes from multiple directions: supply chain disruptions, regulatory threats, attempts to recruit your trained medics away with corporate salaries. Each attack is a test of the system's resilience, and each test teaches you something about what needs to be stronger.

choices.text: Defend the network and press forward
Negotiate instead of confronting. Diplomacy preserves the network

jmed_078.title: The People's Hospital — Part 3
text: The turning point comes when the system saves a corporate executive's child. The family was traveling through the Lower District when the child had an allergic reaction — anaphylaxis, airway closing, minutes from death. The nearest corporate hospital was thirty minutes away. Your satellite clinic was thirty seconds away.
The medic on duty saved the child's life with an epinephrine injection and airway management. Basic medicine, performed competently, in the right place at the right time.

The executive — a woman named Harrington, senior VP at Meridian Industries — arrives at your central clinic the next day. She does not bring lawyers or inspectors. She brings a credstick with enough funds to equip three more satellite clinics and a promise: "Whatever you need. My daughter is alive because of your system."
One patient. One life. The entire calculus changes.

choices.text: Accept the help and use it to cement the network's future
Reject the help. Strings attached to aid become chains

jmed_079.title: The Healer's Legacy
text: The Lower District People's Hospital opens formally on a Tuesday, in the rain, because everything important in this district happens in the rain. It is not one building but twenty-two — a network of clinics, pharmacies, training facilities, and emergency response stations that covers every block in the district.

You stand at the entrance of the central facility and watch patients flow through the doors: workers with injuries, children with fevers, elderly with chronic conditions, and among them, people with cortical stacks and people without, treated with equal competence and equal care.

Okafor watches from a wheelchair, his body failing but his mind — preserved on his stack — still sharp. "You did it," he says. "You built something I dreamed about but never had the courage to attempt."
"You taught me to dream it," you tell him.
The work continues. It always continues. But for the first time, the system works for everyone, and the hands that built it were yours.

jmed_080.title: The Leverage Path — Part 1
text: You take the cure — the EM frequency, the antiviral formulas, the reintegration technique — and you use them as leverage. Not for money, not for power, but for policy change. You contact Dr. Voss at Meridian Medical and make your terms clear.

"I have the cure for the plague. I have the technique for consciousness reintegration. I have evidence linking ClearPoint Solutions to bioterrorism. Here is what I want in exchange: universal healthcare access for the Lower District. No stack registration requirements. No means testing. Full corporate funding for community clinics."
Voss looks at you with an expression that mixes admiration and alarm. "You're blackmailing the most powerful corporation in the city."
"I'm negotiating. Blackmail is when you don't have the moral high ground."

choices.text: Hold firm on all demands
Compromise on minor demands to secure the critical ones

jmed_081.title: The Leverage Path — Part 2
text: The negotiations take weeks. Corporate lawyers, board members, public relations specialists — an army of suits deployed to minimize the damage your demands would cause to their bottom line. You sit across from them with your evidence and your cures and the unshakeable knowledge that they need you more than you need them.

The breakthrough comes when a third plague outbreak threatens — this time in the mid-levels, where corporate employees live. Suddenly the bioweapon isn't a Lower District problem. It's everyone's problem. And you're the only one with the cure.
"Universal access," you repeat. "No stack requirements. Full funding. Or I watch your district die the way you watched mine."
You don't mean it. You would never withhold treatment. But they don't know that, and the bluff holds.

choices.text: Press the advantage while they're vulnerable
Ease up. Pressing too hard invites retaliation

jmed_082.title: The Leverage Path — Part 3
text: They agree. Not to everything — corporations never agree to everything — but to enough. Universal basic healthcare for the Lower District. Community clinics funded by a corporate health tax. Stack registration optional, not required, for medical treatment. An independent oversight board to prevent future bioweapon programs.

The agreement is signed in the Meridian Tower boardroom, and you sit at a table designed for people who wear suits that cost more than your clinic's monthly budget, and you sign your name to a document that will change the lives of a hundred thousand people.

It is not justice. Justice would involve the architects of the plague facing consequences. Justice would involve Hale in a cell. What you've achieved is improvement — systemic, structural, real — and in the Lower District, improvement is the closest thing to justice that exists.

choices.text: Return to the clinic and implement the changes
Celebrate with the clinic staff first. They earned this victory too

jmed_083.title: The Reformer's Legacy
text: You return to the clinic as the doctor who changed the system. The corporate funding flows in — not generously, not without strings, but enough to transform the clinic from a survival operation into a functional medical facility. New equipment. Real medications. Hired staff. The things that should have been available from the beginning.

The community watches the transformation with cautious hope. They have been promised things before. But the clinics open, and the treatment flows, and slowly, carefully, the Lower District begins to believe that healthcare might be a right instead of a privilege.

Okafor sees the changes from his wheelchair. His body is nearly gone, but his mind — backed up or mortal, depending on his choice — is sharp enough to appreciate what you've done. "You played their game," he says. "And you won."
"For now," you say. Because you know corporations. The reforms will erode. The funding will tighten. The promises will fade. But the precedent is set, and precedents are harder to undo than policies.
You are the doctor who forced a corporation to care about the poor. It is not a fairy tale ending. It is a real one.

jmed_084.title: The Exposure Path — Part 1
text: You choose transparency. Everything — the bioweapons, ClearPoint Solutions, Hale's research, the corporate board's involvement, the targeted extermination of unregistered citizens — goes public. You compile the evidence into a data package and transmit it to every independent media outlet, every data haven, every activist network in the city.
The Data Haven's operators warn you: "Once this goes out, you can't take it back. And the people who did this will come for you."
"Let them come. I'm a doctor. I've been fighting death since before they decided to weaponize it."

choices.text: Release the data
Hold the data. Timing the release maximizes impact

jmed_085.title: The Exposure Path — Part 2
text: The data drops and the city ignites. Not literally — though there are fires in the corporate district, set by people whose rage at the revelations exceeds their commitment to property — but informationally. The story spreads through every network, every channel, every conversation. A corporation tried to exterminate the Lower District's unregistered population with engineered plagues. The evidence is undeniable.

Meridian's stock price crashes. Board members resign. ClearPoint Solutions' records are subpoenaed. Dr. Hale goes into hiding. The public outcry is enormous, genuine, and — you fear — temporary. Outrage fades. Memory fades. Systems endure.

But in the immediate aftermath, the attention creates a window. For the first time, the city is looking at the Lower District and seeing people instead of statistics. For the first time, the question 'why don't they have healthcare?' is being asked by people who have the power to answer it.

choices.text: Use the window to push for systemic change while attention holds
Focus on protecting the clinic during the backlash

jmed_086.title: The Exposure Path — Part 3
text: The window is narrow and you use every second. You give interviews. You show reporters the clinic, the satellite facilities, the ledger of names. You put faces to statistics. You make it impossible for the city to look away.

The backlash comes, as you knew it would. Corporate media pivots to character assassination: you're unlicensed, you practice illegal medicine, you've performed unauthorized stack surgeries. All true. All crimes. All committed in service of keeping people alive when the legitimate system refused to.

"Yes," you tell the cameras. "I broke the law. Because the law said these people don't deserve healthcare. Because the law said a child with Kessler's Syndrome should die because the cure costs more than her family earns in a lifetime. I broke the law, and I would do it again, and the question isn't whether I'm a criminal — the question is whether the law that made me one is just."
The speech goes viral. You become a symbol. The movement grows.

choices.text: See it through to the end
Know when to stop pushing. Some battles need to be picked, not fought

jmed_087.title: The Whistleblower's Legacy
text: The reforms come, imperfect and incomplete, as reforms always do. A healthcare access act. An independent investigation into bioweapons programs. Criminal charges against Hale and three board members. Not enough. Never enough. But more than existed before.

You return to the clinic. The media attention fades. The cameras leave. The city's gaze moves to the next crisis, the next outrage, the next burning thing. But the clinic remains, and the patients remain, and you remain.

You are the doctor who told the truth and paid for it with your anonymity, your safety, and the quiet life you might have had. The corporations will never forgive you. The community will never forget you.
Okafor reads the coverage from his wheelchair and smiles — the first time in weeks. "You chose the hardest path," he says. "Exposure means you can never hide again. They'll always know your name."
"Good," you say. "I've been hiding since I started. It's time to practice medicine in the light."

jmed_088.title: The Hunt — Part 1
text: You go after Hale. Not as a doctor but as something older — a person who has watched too many people die from a weapon designed in a lab by a man who will never face the consequences. The legal system won't touch him. Corporate protection will shield him. The only justice available is the kind you deliver yourself.

The decision changes something inside you. You have been a healer for so long that the shift toward something darker feels like a betrayal of every principle Okafor taught you. But Okafor's principles didn't account for a man who builds plagues to kill the poor. Some diseases require excision, not treatment.

You track Hale through corporate records, financial transactions, communication intercepts. He has gone underground, protected by the faction within Meridian that funded his work. Finding him requires skills you don't have — but you know people who do.

choices.text: Call in favors from the community to track Hale
Track Hale through medical supply chains — doctors need equipment

jmed_089.title: The Hunt — Part 2
text: The community responds. Drava, the Bone Saints enforcer you treated years ago, provides muscle. The Data Haven's operators provide intelligence. Kess the pharmaceutical dealer provides access to the black market's information network. Even the transit workers — patients, all of them, people whose lives you saved — provide eyes and ears across the district.
Hale is found in a corporate safe house in the Cloud Towers, protected by private security and the assumption that the Lower District's residents lack the reach to touch him. He is wrong.

You stand outside the safe house with an injection in your pocket. Not poison — you are still a doctor, despite everything. A sedative. Something to put him down quietly so he can be delivered to the people he tried to kill, to face whatever justice they determine.
Or you could use the syringe for something else. Something permanent. Something that would ensure he never builds another plague.

choices.text: Sedate him and bring him back for public trial
End him. Some problems only have one solution

jmed_090.title: The Trial
text: You sedate Hale and bring him back. The trial is held in the clinic's waiting room — not a legal proceeding but a community reckoning. The families of the dead sit in rows of mismatched chairs and look at the man who killed their loved ones.

Hale is articulate, composed, and utterly without remorse. "The unregistered population represents a systemic inefficiency," he says. "Resources consumed without contribution. My work was a solution to a demographic problem."
The silence that follows is the most dangerous sound you've ever heard. A room full of people who have just been told they are a problem to be solved, by the man who tried to solve them with plague.

The community votes. Not on guilt — that is beyond question — but on consequence. Some want death. Some want exile. Some want him to work in the clinic, treating the people he tried to kill, for the rest of his life.

choices.text: Advocate for mercy — imprisonment, not death
Let the community decide without your influence

jmed_091.title: The Doctor's Choice
text: You stand over Hale with the syringe and you think about Okafor's first lesson: treat them like they matter, because they do. You think about every life you've saved and every life you've lost. You think about the hippocratic oath you never formally took but have lived by since your first night in Block 14.
First, do no harm.

You look at Hale, and you see a man whose continued existence threatens every person you've sworn to protect. A man who will build more plagues if given the chance. A man the system will never punish because the system protects its own.
You put the syringe down. Not because you can't do it, but because if you do, you become something other than a doctor. And the district needs a doctor more than it needs an executioner.
You sedate him with the original dose and carry him out.

choices.text: Bring him back for the community to decide
Treat his injuries first. You're a doctor before anything else

jmed_092.title: Justice, Such As It Is
text: The community decides. Hale is imprisoned in the Lower District — not killed, not exiled, but held. He will spend his remaining years in a cell made from the same shipping containers his victims lived in, breathing the same recycled air, eating the same protein rations. The symmetry is intentional.

It is not justice. Justice is an abstraction that requires a functioning legal system, which the Lower District does not have. What it is, is consequence — the first time a corporate architect of suffering has faced any consequence at all, imposed not by the law but by the people he tried to eliminate.

The precedent resonates throughout the city. The corporations take notice. The Lower District is no longer a population that can be quietly erased. It is a community that fights back, that holds its enemies accountable, that has a doctor who will not let them die without a fight.

choices.text: Return to the clinic. The work continues
Stay and see justice through. Walking away too early leaves loose ends

jmed_093.title: The Avenger's Legacy
text: You return to the clinic with blood on your hands — not literal blood, but the metaphorical stain of a doctor who stepped outside the boundaries of healing to pursue something harder and darker and necessary. You hunted a man. You brought him to justice. You did not kill him, but you were willing to, and that willingness has changed you in ways you are still discovering.

The clinic continues. The patients continue. The work is the same as it has always been — wounds to close, illnesses to treat, lives to save, lives to lose. But you carry it differently now. Not with the idealism of the street doc who sewed up his first patient with fishing line, but with the weighted determination of someone who has seen the full scope of what medicine means in a world that uses disease as a weapon.

Okafor watches you work and says nothing about the change. Perhaps he doesn't see it. Perhaps he sees it and recognizes it from his own journey — from corporate surgeon to fugitive to clinic doctor to mentor. The path is different, but the destination is the same: a person who has done terrible and beautiful things in service of keeping other people alive.

You are the doctor who fought back. The healer who became a hunter and then became a healer again. The legacy is complicated. The patients don't care about legacies. They care that you're here, and your hands are steady, and the clinic's light is on.

jmed_094.title: Emergency Protocol
text: A mass transit accident floods the clinic with casualties. Seventy-three injured, twelve critical. Your staff mobilizes with drilled precision, but the volume exceeds capacity. You make rapid-fire triage decisions, directing patients, allocating resources, performing emergency surgery between management calls.
Among the critical is a corporate security officer — the same kind of officer who enforces the laws you break daily. He is bleeding out, and his colleagues watch you with weapons visible.
You treat him. Not because he deserves it more than anyone else, but because he doesn't deserve it less. That's what being a doctor means: the wound is the only credential that matters.

choices.text: Continue managing the mass casualty event
Delegate triage to your trained staff. You can't do everything yourself

jmed_095.title: The Corporate Officer
text: The officer survives. His name is Torres, and he returns to consciousness with the disoriented confusion of someone waking in enemy territory. He looks at the clinic — its improvised equipment, its unlicensed staff, its patients who include the very unregistered citizens he's paid to monitor — and his expression cycles through confusion, recognition, and something that might be shame.
"Thank you," he says. Two words that bridge the gap between the system he enforces and the system you've built to replace it.

Torres becomes an unlikely ally. He doesn't change the system from within — that's a fantasy — but he stops enforcing it against your clinic. Inspections are missed. Reports are misfiled. A small act of individual decency in an indecent system. It's not much. It's enough.

choices.text: Accept his help quietly and keep working
Refuse his help publicly. Corporate involvement taints the clinic

jmed_096.title: Training the Next Generation
text: You begin training new doctors. Not the formal education of a medical academy — you don't have the resources or the legitimacy — but the practical training that produces competent physicians from determined people. You teach the way Okafor taught you: through practice, through crisis, through the relentless pressure of a patient load that never ends.

Your first graduating class is six students. A former gang runner with steady hands. A mother of four who taught herself anatomy from a stolen textbook. A synth whose diagnostic capabilities surpass your own. A teenager who watched you save her father and decided she wanted to do the same.

You give them the same speech Okafor gave you: "The work never stops. The patients never end. And the system that creates them never changes. If you're looking for a career that makes you feel good about the world, this is not it."
They stay anyway. All six of them.

choices.text: Send them to establish clinics across the district
Keep the best ones at the main clinic. Quality over coverage

jmed_097.title: Okafor's End
text: Okafor dies on a quiet morning. No crisis, no drama, no emergency. His body simply stops, the way bodies do when they have been fighting for too long. You are there, holding his hand, monitoring vital signs that you already know are terminal.

His last words, depending on the choice he made, are either irrelevant — his consciousness preserved on his stack, his death merely a transition — or absolute: the final thoughts of a mind that chose mortality and accepted its weight.

Either way, the clinic feels different afterward. Smaller. Quieter. The space he occupied — not physically but gravitationally, the way certain people warp the world around them by their presence — remains empty.

You write his name in the ledger. The last name, for now. Not because death has stopped but because the ledger is full, and you need a new one. You start a new ledger with a blank first page that you know will not stay blank for long.

choices.text: Grieve. Then return to work. That's what he would want
Take time to grieve properly. Rushing back to work dishonors his memory

jmed_098.title: The Work Continues
text: The next patient arrives before the grief has settled. A woman with contractions, eight months along, complications evident from the way she's breathing — too fast, too shallow, pain mixed with fear. Her partner hovers behind her with the helpless urgency of someone who cannot do the one thing that matters.

You wash your hands, glove up, and go to work. The delivery is complicated — breech presentation, cord compression, the kind of case that requires every skill you've developed over your career. You work with the calm precision that Okafor taught you, the calm that says I have done this before and I will do it again and right now, in this moment, nothing exists except this patient and this procedure.

The baby is born at 3:47 AM. A girl. She screams immediately, which is the most beautiful sound in medicine — the sound of lungs that work, of a body that intends to survive, of a life beginning in a district where too many lives end too soon.
You hand the baby to her mother and for a moment — just a moment — the weight lifts.

choices.text: Step outside. Watch the rain. Then go back in
Stay inside. The clinic needs you more than the rain does

jmed_099.title: Rain
text: You stand outside the clinic in the rain. The Lower District stretches around you — towers of shipping containers, flickering neon, the distant hum of transit lines, the smell of machine oil and antiseptic and rain on metal. This is your district. These are your people. This is the work that will never finish.

Inside the clinic, the lights are on. Through the window, you can see Lin checking on the new mother. Ravi calibrating equipment. The junior medics reviewing charts. A waiting room with patients who need you. A world that needs fixing in ways that medicine alone cannot address.

You think about Okafor. About the fishing line and the first patient. About the plague and the antivenom and the child you couldn't save or the child you did. About Kenji on the table. About Dmitri waking up. About every hand you've held and every wound you've closed and every name in the ledger.
The rain falls. The clinic's light shines. You go back inside.

choices.text: There is always one more patient
Rest. Tomorrow there will be patients. Tonight there is peace

jmed_100.title: One More Patient
text: The next patient is a child. He has a cough. His mother looks worried in the way that mothers look worried — completely, cellularly, with every atom of her being oriented toward the small person she brought into a world that does not deserve him.
You kneel to the child's height. "Let me hear that cough," you say.
He coughs. It is a small, ordinary cough. Not the cough of plague or Kessler's Syndrome or chemical burns. Just a cough. A cold, probably. Rest, fluids, time. The simplest prescription in medicine.

You write it out anyway. You hand it to the mother with the same care you'd give a surgical treatment plan. Because this is what Okafor taught you, what the street taught you, what every patient and every crisis and every impossible choice taught you: every patient matters. Every cough matters. Every life that walks through your door deserves the full weight of your attention and your skill and your care.
"He'll be fine," you tell the mother.
She smiles. The child smiles. And for a moment, in a clinic in the Lower District, in a world that has done everything it can to make these people disposable, everything is fine.
The next patient is waiting.
